At the current valuation, the market appears to be overly-rotated on near-term headwinds from elevated supply in Sunbelt markets. However, as the earlier graph indicated, lower supply in upcoming quarters could bode well for more meaningful SSNOI growth for UDR. Over the medium term, I would expect UDR to regain a 5-7% FFO/share growth annually as supply moderates. This, combined with the 4.4% yield and potential for a reversion to the mean valuation, could result in market-beating total returns from here.

Risks to UDR include potential for continued oversupply in some key markets like Texas, which may impact the timing of a near-term recovery in rental rate growth. Moreover, the potential for new rent control ordinances in select markets in Seattle and San Francisco could impact UDR’s pricing power. Lastly, higher inflation could raise construction costs and impact initial yields on investments.

Investor Takeaway

UDR continues to stand out as a high-quality multifamily REIT trading at a discounted valuation. It’s demonstrating steady operating fundamentals with above-guidance revenue and NOI growth, improved tenant retention, and disciplined cost management. UDR is well-positioned to return to mid-single-digit FFO growth as supply headwinds are expected to ease. It’s supported by a strong balance sheet and has investment opportunities at attractive yields.

Lastly, UDR’s reliable dividend is backed by a conservative payout ratio and investment-grade credit. UDR qualifies as a long-term compounder that can be had at an attractive price due to short-term challenges that can be resolved. As such, I continue to rate UDR as a ‘Buy’ for value and income.

data.title: Market Valuations Don't Matter... Until They Do
data.description:
Market Brief – J-Pow Sends Market Back To Highs

After the market slid lower all week, testing the 20-DMA on Thursday, Jerome Powell’s speech at Jackson Hole turned sentiment on a dime. The S&P 500 returned to record highs, and the Dow surged 900 points. Investors took his tone as confirmation that a September rate cut is in play. Specifically, Powell noted the slowdown in employment as a key reason for the shift in stance, as noted in the key paragraph from his speech.

“In the near term, risks to inflation are tilted to the upside, and risks to employment to the downside—a challenging situation. When our goals are in tension like this, our framework calls for us to balance both sides of our dual mandate.

Our policy rate is now 100 basis points closer to neutral than it was a year ago, and the stability of the unemployment rate and other labor market measures allows us to proceed carefully as we consider changes to our policy stance.

Nonetheless, with policy in restrictive territory, the baseline outlook and the shifting balance of risks may warrant adjusting our policy stance.“

Wall Street also noticed the shift in tone. Krishna Guha of Evercore ISI called it a “clear pivot to risk management.” He added the speech “gave markets permission to rally.” Bank of America’s Michael Gapen noted Powell’s comments were “dovish without overcommitting,” keeping pressure off the Fed to act immediately while softening market fears.

While stocks had been under pressure earlier this week, specifically in the overbought Technology sector, Powell opened the door for the bulls. The rally lifted risk-based sectors. Small caps led. Bitcoin and bonds bid, and homebuilders, airlines, and regional banks surged. The dollar fell as yields dropped sharply.

However, risks remain. Inflation is not yet anchored. Powell downplayed tariff impacts, but new trade policies could stoke price pressures. If data surprises to the upside, markets could whiplash. BlackRock’s Rick Rieder warned the Fed “must still see inflation trending toward target,” and cautioned that easing too fast “could reignite instability.” Don Carter at Fort Washington Investors also remained concerned, stating, “Even with Powell’s more dovish tone, the data in the coming weeks still pose some risks. The market will like this change in tone. But I think we must be careful not to get too far ahead. There is still a lot of data between now and the next FOMC meeting.”

As we will discuss in today’s newsletter, while earnings season was solid, valuations are clearly stretched. The risk to investors is that with the Fed’s next move priced in, any hesitation could lead to stocks giving back recent gains.

Technical Backdrop

Price remains in a primary uptrend as the S&P 500 held its initial test of the 20-DMA on Thursday. On a daily basis, the market remains well above the 20, 50, and 200 DMAs, with the top of the current trend channel rising toward 6600. Such keeps the trend constructive as long as pullbacks hold support. Short‑term pivots for the next session cluster around 6,470, with resistance being the recent all-time highs from two weeks ago.

Furthermore, daily momentum is positive but edging hot. RSI(14) sits in the low‑60s, MACD, however, remains on a “sell,” and several faster oscillators (Stoch/RSI variants) registered overbought readings into Friday’s rebound. One note is that relative strength, momentum, and money flows remain in a “negative divergence” to the market, increasing near-term investor risk. However, the “buy‑the‑dip” strategy continues to perform well for now.

Participation improved, but it’s not euphoric. Roughly 61% of S&P constituents are above their 50‑DMA and about 63% above their 200‑DMA, which is healthy, but not yet stretched. For now, breadth is supportive, but still something to monitor for confirmation via higher highs in the A/D lines.

Despite the sell-off this past week, volatility remained subdued and closed near 14 into the close, consistent with a risk‑on term structure. Options appetite is still very bullish: the 10‑day average Cboe total put/call sits near 0.84. Positioning is mixed across cohorts: NAAIM equity exposure remains high while AAII shows a cautious retail stance. After this week’s close, we will see if retail begins to chase more aggressively.

Under the surface, the week’s rebound broadened: small caps and cyclical pockets outperformed on Friday, with the Russell 2000 ripping ~4% as yields fell. Equal‑weight (RSP) has begun to show short‑term relative improvement versus cap‑weight (NYSEARCA:SPY). Even though RSP still trails YTD, it is an encouraging sign of incremental broadening. Sector‑wise, weekly performance tilted toward cyclicals (energy/materials/industrials) with defensives mixed, while megacap tech cooled earlier in the week and rejoined late.

Key Levels & Risk Markers (near‑term)

  • Support: 6,385 (20‑DMA), 6,269 (50‑DMA), 6,269 (200‑DMA). A close below the 50‑DMA would signal loss of short‑term trend grip.
  • Momentum guardrails: Daily RSI mid‑60s; a rollover with negative divergence would raise pullback odds.
  • Internals to confirm: S&P %>50‑DMA pushing sustainably >65–70%.
  • Sentiment: Watch if VIX ≤13 and put/call MA pushes lower, would increase risk of a shakeout.

Outlook for Next Week: Neutral

The primary trend is up (price above rising key MAs; breadth decent), and Friday’s broad snap‑back helps. But short‑term momentum is warm, volatility is compressed, and pro exposure is already high while seasonality turns less friendly. Netting those, the risk/reward into next week looks Neutral: tactically favor pullback buys above the 20‑DMA and fade crowded breakouts if internals (breadth/AD lines) fail to confirm. Keep an eye on small‑cap follow‑through and whether equal‑weight leadership persists; both would upgrade the signal; a quick VIX spike and breadth roll‑over would downgrade it.

Key Catalysts Next Week

Next week’s calendar could test the durability of the post-Jackson Hole market rally. The Fed’s preferred inflation gauge, the July PCE price index, is set for release alongside data on personal income and consumer spending. These figures will help shape expectations for the September FOMC meeting. On the earnings side, Nvidia reports midweek, with investors looking for signs of continued AI-driven growth. Reports from MongoDB, CrowdStrike, and HP will also offer insight into tech sector momentum. Retail names like Best Buy, Bath & Body Works, and Urban Outfitters will give a read on discretionary spending trends.

Overall Risk Outlook: Neutral

The short-term outlook leans neutral, but with a bullish bias if key data confirms a soft-landing narrative. Markets are coming off a strong rebound driven by Powell’s dovish tone at Jackson Hole. His signal that the Fed may ease if labor market risks rise gave investors a reason to buy back into risk assets. That optimism now hinges on incoming data. Investors should stay alert. The bias is bullish, but a neutral posture is warranted unless data aligns with dovish policy expectations.

Market Valuations Don’t Matter, Until They Do

One of the hallmarks of very late-stage bull market cycles is the inevitable bashing of long-term market valuation metrics. In the late 90s, if you were buying shares of Berkshire Hathaway, it was mocked as “driving Dad’s old Pontiac.” In 2007, valuation metrics were dismissed because the markets were flush with liquidity, low interest rates, and “Subprime was contained.”

“Valuation is the capstone of proximate causes for a market top, and the one most indicative of the potential magnitude of any subsequent selloff. It’s well known that valuations are high for the US market, but I thought I’d update my aggregate indicator, which combines the main measures of long-term stock-market worth. It previously peaked in April, but has just made a new all-time high this month. Not a welcome sign if you’re a long-term bull.” – Simon White, Bloomberg

Of course, just as we have seen so many times, we again see repeated arguments about why “this time is different.” For some, it is the belief that the Fed will bail out markets if something goes wrong. For others, “Artificial Intelligence” and “Cryptocurrencies” are a new paradigm of investment returns. Of course, it is hard to blame investors for feeling this way, given the market’s outsized gains over the last 15 years.

Regardless of the reasoning, there is little argument that current trailing market valuations are elevated.

However, we need to understand two crucial points about valuations.

  1. Market valuations are not a catalyst for mean reversions, and;
  2. They are a terrible market timing tool.

Furthermore, investors often overlook the most essential aspects of valuations.

  1. Valuations are excellent predictors of return on 10 and 20-year periods, and;
  2. They are the fuel for mean-reverting events.

Critics argue that valuations have been high for quite some time, and a market reversion hasn’t occurred. However, to our point above, valuation models are not “market timing indicators.” The vast majority of analysts assume that if a measure of valuation (P/E, P/S, P/B, etc.) reaches some specific level, it means that:

  1. The market is about to crash, and;
  2. Investors should be in 100% cash.

This is incorrect.

Market valuation measures are just that—a measure of current valuation. Moreover, market valuations are a much better measure of “investor psychology” and a manifestation of the “greater fool theory.” This is why a high correlation exists between one-year trailing valuations and consumer confidence in higher stock prices.

What market valuations express should be obvious. If you “overpay” for something today, the future net return will be lower than if you had paid a discount for it.

Current market valuations are not sustainable. Fundamentals, revenue growth, earnings power, free cash flow, margins, and debt govern valuation over time. This is particularly true when the vast majority of the market generates little to no earnings growth, but growth is only a function of a handful of companies.

Markets eventually will revert toward fundamentals. That process takes time, but it is both inevitable and relentless.

Price‑to‑Sales and Market‑Cap‑to‑GDP Send a Warning

The Price‑to‑Sales (P/S) ratio measures how much investors pay for each dollar of a company’s sales. The S&P 500 currently trades around 3.2 times trailing sales. The long‑term average is closer to 1.6 times. For perspective, a P/S ratio above “2″ signals elevated valuations. The market P/S ratio is currently more than 2-standard deviation above its historic average.

The elevated P/S reflects bullish expectations that when you pay over $3 per $1 of sales, you expect future growth to justify it. That means investors expect strong revenue gains ahead. But if growth slows, valuations must adjust downward. In other words, the market is currently “priced for perfection,“ which leaves a lot of room for disappointment.

Another measure is Market‑Cap‑to‑GDP, known as the Buffett indicator. This measure compares total stock market value to national output. Given that earnings and revenue growth come from economic activity, the market valuation should represent the strength of the overall economy. Currently, that measure of market valuation resides at 217%. Notably, the long‑term average is around 155%. At current levels, valuations are well above what the economy can generate, and two standard deviations above the long-term trend.

That signals broad market overvaluation versus economic size. It suggests prices may be disconnected from the real economy that generates earnings.

Both metrics send a clear message: valuations exceed long‑term norms. That means excess return potential is limited. Downside risk rises if sentiment shifts or fundamentals falter.

These high valuations can be sustained longer than expected if sentiment remains jubilant. But you cannot ignore the math. Expectations already baked into the price are high. Therefore, you must realize that you tolerate a limited margin of safety unless fundamentals outperform.

Valuation Exuberance Increases The Overall Risk Profile

Still, the current level of exuberance is unsurprising given the strongly trending bull market, particularly when Wall Street needs to justify higher valuations. The problem is that such exuberant forecasts rarely come to fruition. For example, in March 2023, S&P Global predicted that 2024 earnings would grow by 13% for the year. In reality, earnings grew by just 9% despite the market rising nearly 28%. In other words, given that actual earnings fell well short of previous estimates, the 2024 market was primarily driven by valuation expansion.

Current earnings projections for 2025 suggest a nearly 20% increase, well above historical growth trends. While such detachments of the market from earnings are not uncommon, they tend not to be sustainable over more extended periods. We suspect that the risk to stocks in 2025 will be a failure of earnings to meet optimistic expectations.

When sentiment and expectations exceed economic realities, there is the potential for stock repricing. As noted, “stocks are priced for perfection,” which means any shortfall could lead to a more substantial decline in price. For instance, the S&P 500’s P/E ratio has reached levels that some analysts consider concerning, reflecting investor optimism that may not align with underlying economic fundamentals.

Given the interdependence between earnings and economic growth, valuations present a more serious challenge. A better way to visualize this data is to look at the correlation between the annual change in earnings growth and inflation-adjusted GDP. There are periods when earnings deviate from underlying economic activity. However, those periods are due to pre- or post-recession earnings fluctuations. Currently, economic and earnings growth are very close to the long-term correlation.

It is worth repeating that valuations are unreliable market-timing tools. Elevated valuations reflect heightened investor optimism and expectations of robust earnings growth in bull markets and can remain that way for extended periods.

However, excess market valuations leave investors vulnerable to unexpected, exogenous events. Those “events,” when they occur, lead to sharp sentiment reversals. What would cause such a sentiment reversal? No one knows. This is why when the “unexpected” happens, Wall Street’s immediate response is to suggest that “no one could have seen that coming.”

As such, investors must continue managing risk into 2025 and navigate the markets accordingly.

Portfolio Tactics – Investing During High Valuation Periods

High valuations hurt long‑term returns. Elevated P/S and market‑cap‑to‑GDP ratios reduce future upside. They increase downside risk if sentiment reverses. You still can participate. You still can protect.

Consider these tactics as you consider your current exposure.

  1. Trim exposure at stretched multiples: When forward P/E tops 22 or P/S surpasses 3.2×, scale back positions. Take profit. Lock gains while fundamentals remain intact.
  2. Add value sectors: Move into energy and healthcare. These sectors often trade at lower multiples and typically offer more substantial dividends. They may provide defensive ballast.
  3. Hold cash or short‑duration bonds: Preserve optionality. Limit risk from pullbacks. You stay in the game without full exposure.
  4. Rotate to international and small‑cap value: U.S. valuations are richly priced. Value opportunities may exist overseas or in small companies. That offers diversification and lower price risk.
  5. Employ hedges or protective strategies: Covered calls, tail hedges, and partial inverse ETFs can provide downside support when sentiment peaks.
  6. Rebalance using valuation-based rules: Set thresholds for P/S and market-cap‑to‑GDP. When these thresholds are breached, allocate to defensive assets. This maintains discipline.
  7. Watch fundamentals closely: Track earnings yield versus bond yield, free cash flow margins, and interest coverage. If fundamental health deteriorates, reduce exposure further. Let data guide your moves.

These tactics let you ride the rally. They also protect when sentiment fades. They give you balance while participating with caution.

Trade accordingly.

Market Statistics & Analysis

Market & Sector X-Ray: Overbought

Last week, we warned investors to be mindful of the technical deterioration, which led to a sell-off early in the week. However, Friday’s surge toward all-time highs sent most markets and sectors back to extreme overbought conditions. While exuberance may feed over into next week, we recommend using any further gains to reduce overbought positions, trim out weak positions, and rebalance overall risk.

Technical Composite: 86.64 – Very Overbought

Despite the market weakness early in the week, the sharp rally on Friday pushed the majority of technical indicators back to weekly overbought conditions. While the correction to the 20-DMA was healthy, it failed to reverse conditions for a more sustainable rally.

Fear/Greed Index: 74.79 – Greed Increases With Rally

Despite the early week correction, the market rally on Friday kept the fear/greed gauge near extreme greed levels, rising from 71.22 to 74.79. While the increase was mild, it continues to suggest a more aggressive posture by investors in the markets, which keeps a larger corrective process from occurring. In other words, investors continue to aggressively “buy dips.”

Relative Sector Performance

Last week, we noted:

“That rotation began this past week, with Healthcare performing well, along with Financials and Discretionary. Technology showed some signs of weakness as money rotated to more fundamentally based sectors. Keep a watch on Real Estate and Energy, which are very oversold and out of favor. The next chart shows some energy names to watch.”

The weakness continued early this week, with healthcare moving sharply higher. Real estate also performed well, and money sought more defensive positioning amid the technology sector slide. The good news is that technology is now oversold, which caught a decent rotation trade back into those key names, as shown in the next chart, on Friday.

Most Oversold Sectors

As noted above, Technology is now the most oversold sector, and on Friday, money returned to names like AVGO, PLTR, and NVDA.

Sector Model & Risk Ranges

The early-week correction failed to reverse the market’s more overbought conditions. Notably, the Friday rally pushed numerous sectors and markets well above the monthly risk ranges, suggesting that further upside is likely limited until there is a bit of a reset. The sectors with double-digit deviations from their long-term means are at the most significant risk of corrective action. Take profits in those sectors and look to rotate to more oversold areas of the market.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Viasat: Growth Depends On How Well It Executes
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In this article, I am going to explore Viasat Inc. (VSAT). Viasat operates in the satellite communications industry, and it recently grabbed my attention.

Viasat appears undervalued, particularly when we do a peer comparison and consider its cash flow and profits.

This implies that any enhancements in the way the company operates would increase its value considerably. However, it's not as simple as it sounds.

Viasat burns a lot of money, has a significant debt, and faces risks with the satellite launches and integration.

I do not expect the stock to come off the discount until I see consistent cash flow and reduced debt. Therefore, the business has to execute smartly so that the upside potential can materialize.

Before I proceed to numbers, we should understand how the company works.

Spoiler: I am assigning VSAT stock a Hold rating right now.

Company Overview

Viasat is a worldwide communications company that was founded in 1986 in Carlsbad, California. With time, the company has become a major force in satellite communications.

Currently, Viasat operates in three sectors.

The first is Satellite Services, which are used to deliver internet and connectivity to homes, airplanes, ships, and remote locations.

The second is Government Systems that provide secure communications, data links, and ground equipment to the U.S. Department of Defense and allied governments.

Then there is Commercial Networks, through which Viasat constructs and markets satellite payloads, antennas, and networking hardware to other operators.

The distinguishing feature of Viasat is that it does everything on its own.

The company plans and constructs satellites, installs ground systems, and operates the network that provides the service.

This method maintains costs, increases quality, and helps to introduce new services faster.

This can be observed in the Viasat-3 program, which is set to provide stronger international coverage and higher capacity, although the initial launch in 2023 faced some deployment problems.

Viasat also has a broad base of customers.

Its services cater to households, airlines, shipping companies, energy firms, businesses, and governments worldwide.

Moreover, the Inmarsat deal has strengthened its global mobility business, and its long government contracts provide it a decent source of income.

Having seen how Viasat operates, it is time to assess the numbers and see how the company is actually performing.

A Revenue Mix Shift Toward Predictability

In Q1 FY2026, Viasat had a revenue of $1.2 billion, which is 4% more than the previous year’s $1.13 billion.

Although this revenue growth is not much, the revenue mix is a better indicator to assess Viasat’s performance.

The combination is shifting to recurring services.

Communication Services was essentially flat at $827 million and now contributes to around 70% of the total revenue, with aviation and government satcom as the mainstays.

On the other hand, the product revenue declined by about 17% following the Energy Services Systems Integration divestiture.

Notably, the Defense and Advanced Technologies [DAT] increased by 15% to $344 million, providing most of the growth.

Aviation is expanding to cover over 4,100 commercial aircraft and approximately 2,050 business jets. Whereas, fixed broadband continues to decline, with 172,000 subs and a steady ARPU of $115.

The result is a more robust revenue base with fewer hardware fluctuations, but one with its execution risk focused on aviation installs and defense program delivery.

As long as these areas can keep ramping, revenue will be easier to predict. If they stumble, then headline growth will fall short.

Profitability Split Between Scale and Heavy Investment

Profitability shows the same division between scale and investment. Adjusted EBITDA was 34.5%, or $408 million, almost flat YOY.

Communication Services produced about 39% margins. It means that Viasat earns a healthy revenue from subscriptions and smart utilization of the network.

Meanwhile, DAT reported a margin of 25%, less than the norm due to large expenditures on R&D and less revenue generation from licensing its intellectual property.

On a trailing basis, the profile looks the same: EBITDA margin of 30.1%, EBIT margin between 1-2%, and a negative net income margin.

This gap is typical of a capital-intensive satellite model where depreciation and amortization of Inmarsat and Viasat-3 move operating costs into the red, but still operate profitably.

This is why cash flow explains the scenario better than EPS does.

Cash Flow Gains Still at the Mercy of Capex

Cash conversion has been better, but the company will have to demonstrate longer-term sustainability.

The operating cash flow clocked in at $258 million, capex was reduced to $198 million, with free cash flow of $60 million.

The liquidity stands at a comfortable position of $2.3 billion, both in cash and available credit. The redemption of approximately $443 million in 2025 notes decreases short-term refinancing risk.

Its net debt is about $5.6 billion. On an annualized basis, adjusted EBITDA is around $1.63 billion, so leverage is 3.4x, which is not too bad as long as free cash flow holds.

However, the durability of the cash flow is a concern. Viasat converted 15% of EBITDA to free cash flow in the quarter, but the trailing conversion is more like 5%.

The company anticipates that the free cash flow will be positive continuously in the second half of FY2026.

At the same time, Capex remains close to 20% of sales, and asset turnover is about 0.29x, which makes the business too capital-intensive to provide sustainable cash generation in the near term.

The liquidity cushion of $2.3 billion and reasonable leverage give it time, but the structural problem is that excessive volatility in quarterly free cash will continue until the investment cycle in satellites slows down. A consistent generation of free cash has a slim chance of occurring until 2026.

How Viasat's Valuation Multiples Compare To Peers

The valuation difference is pronounced when I compare Viasat with companies such as CommScope (COMM), Calix (CALX), and Extreme Networks (EXTR).

Viasat trades at a forward EV/EBITDA of 6x.

When we look across its peers, COMM, CALX, and EXTR trade at 9.8x, 12.9x, and 31.5x, respectively.

This suggests that Viasat is not only slightly cheaper, but it trades at less than half the peer average.

We see a similar trend in forward sales multiples. Viasat EV/S multiple is at 2x, which is pretty close to COMM and EXTR, but it is still way lower compared to Calix, which is more than 3.5x.

The discount becomes clearer when we look at the trailing multiples.

Viasat trades at a low P/CF of 3.4x. CommScope is almost 12x, Extreme is just over 17x, and Calix is at 42x.

Additionally, the P/S and P/B ratios also show how wide the gap actually is. VSAT’s P/S and P/B ratios are at 0.74x and 0.76x, respectively. On the other hand, its peers are trading at much higher valuations.

This indicates that the market is not ignoring Viasat but is significantly discounting it.

Viasat Valuation Path: Opportunities and Risks

For a heavy-spending business like Viasat, EV/EBITDA and cash-flow multiples are the relevant lenses. In the meantime, P/E is not a good guide here. Intensive depreciation and amortization, coupled with significant interest expenses, leave pre-tax earnings modest in the near term. That is why we can see the non-GAAP P/E of ~337x on FY2 estimates. Such a figure is mostly noise in this model.

A reference to the current market facts keeps the story concrete: currently at ~$26.10 and 134.3 million shares, the company’s market value is roughly $3.5 billion.

Enterprise value is $9.1 billion and net debt is $5.6 billion. That suggests an efficient forward EBITDA of $1.52 billion (EV ÷ 6x).

In other words, every per unit of movement in EV/EBITDA is equivalent to $1.52 billion of EV, or $11.3 per share, so incremental changes in the multiple have massive implications on equity value.

Due to that sensitivity, execution has become the de facto measure of valuation. Viasat, with a steady EBITDA, a well-managed capex, and visibly lowered debt, would generate upside even with modest multiple growth.

For example, assume the EBITDA is constant at the implied ~9x multiple of $60 per share. Alternatively, assuming the free cash flow stabilizes at ~$0.5 to 0.7B after capex and the market begins to use a P/FCF ratio of ~15x, the market capitalization will be ~$10 billion (= $74.5 per share).

Such scenarios clarify the mechanics: Cash conversion and visible deleveraging are the necessary stimuli to a re-rating.

On the other hand, the drawback is straightforward too.

A 5.5x multiple, based on the same EBITDA, would reduce EV to ~$8.34 billion and equity to ~$2.74 billion, or a share price of about $20.

A further squeeze to 5x brings the stock to the teens. In brief, modest shifts in the speed of conversion of cash or investor attitude towards leverage have large share-price effects.

Two clear catalysts can alter the math materially, as already discussed in the piece above.

The first is the ViaSat-3 satellite program, which will increase the network capacity of the company by more than twice.

The second satellite (F2) is under preparation for shipment, and the third (F3) is under environmental testing.

If launches and deployments continue to go according to schedule, Viasat will have an opportunity to generate new revenue at high incremental margins. Any delays would set growth back even more as costs keep running.

Then there is the Ligado agreement. If the court approves, it would authorize a maximum of $568 million in FY2026, in two payments, beginning in September.

This would cut net debt by around 10%. At a 7x EV/EBITDA re-rating, that alone would be worth $4-5 per share (568 million ÷ 134.3 million = ~ 4.2).

The effect of management guidance excluding Ligado proceeds is that any court approval would be an add-on upside to the above scenarios.

Bottom Line

The real investor question is not whether 6x is cheap or expensive in a vacuum, but whether Viasat can achieve a multiple-quarter pattern of (1) stability or increased adjusted EBITDA, (2) repeatable free cash flow as capex normalizes, and (3) debt paydown evidence.

Achieving all of this is pretty challenging in the near term.

When these things occur, the multiple math will take care of the rest. Until then, the stock will remain highly sensitive to both progress and setbacks.

The stock has good growth potential, but it is extremely contingent on execution.

It will not be an instant process to experience the stable free cash flow and considerable debt reduction due to high capex and constant project risks.

At this point, a Hold rating is reasonable, as the company deserves to have time to show that it can provide consistent performance.

data.title: My #1 Place To Buy Undervalued Income - And 3 Picks I'm Betting On Now
data.description:
Introduction

From a real estate standpoint, he says, there’s no better bet than America. “It doesn’t matter the administration, doesn’t matter the president—it’s always the US.

I was absolutely fascinated when I read the quote above in a recent article published by Bloomberg, as it basically reflects how I feel about investing in the United States in general.

As most of my readers may know, I have 100% of my portfolio invested in North American stocks, with the overwhelming majority of that capital being invested in the U.S., a market I always preferred over non-U.S. markets due to factors I highlighted in an article titled "Red, White, And Wealth: Building An All-American Dividend Portfolio."

While the U.S. housing market, in general, has hit a wall of affordability issues, including elevated rates, high home prices, and poor consumer sentiment, foreign buyers are rushing back, making 2025 the first year of higher foreign real estate investments since 2017, as we can see in the chart below.

Although foreign buyers account for only 2.5% of existing real estate housing deals, they are an increasing force, supported by less competition from U.S. buyers, as most foreign buyers are all-cash buyers who are, on average, in better financial shape than American buyers.

After all, someone who deliberately invests overseas is very different from an "average" American who's simply looking to buy a house for their family.

With that said, although there are certainly reasons not to invest in real estate, I believe the poor sentiment creates tremendous opportunities, as I wrote in an article titled "The Only 2 Places I'd Put Big Money For Income And Growth Right Now."

I brought up three reasons to consider real estate investments, all of which are related in some way:

1. Recent years were way below average

As we can see below, the past few years were quite bad for REITs, as the average annual return for the 2020-2024 period was just 5.5%, a mile below the 12.4% average annual return going back to the early 1990s.

While this doesn't mean we're in for a sudden rebound, it certainly indicates that the odds for successful investments are in our favor, as REITs have never had a period of subdued returns for more than four years.

2. Valuations are very attractive

Currently, REIT equities are trading at the biggest discount (6.5x) to the S&P 500 since the Great Financial Crisis, if we exclude the pandemic. Ever since inflation peaked at the end of 2022, it has been nonstop underperformance for REITs, as they simply could not keep up with the tech-heavy S&P 500. Besides that, the high rates on risk-free government bonds caused a lot of capital to flow into bonds instead of REITs, which are also income-focused investments.

Generally speaking, REITs do not trade at a huge discount to the market.

3. Cheap valuations don't last

The average total return following a period of REIT discounts of more than -2.0x (the current discount is 6.5x) is substantially higher for REITs than U.S. equities, in general, as we can see below. Looking back two decades, we can expect at least two years of REIT outperformance, technically speaking.

But wait, there's more!

In a market with a rather lofty valuation, real estate can function as a great alternative, as it has a very low correlation to U.S. stocks. While even value and small-cap stocks have a long-term correlation of more than 0.85, REITs have a correlation of roughly 0.65, making them excellent diversification tools.

Moreover, despite rising rates, REITs have done tremendously well, as their net operating income has consistently made new highs this year.

With that said, in this market, a few income-focused areas stand out to me.

One of them is the energy midstream industry, which benefits from fixed-fee contracts from pipeline and related support companies. I absolutely love that industry, as I am bullish on both energy supply and demand and the need to build new infrastructure in areas like the Permian Basin.

However, REITs have a benefit that midstream companies do not have, which is a better valuation and a history of consistency. See, while subdued oil prices do not hurt most midstream companies, whenever oil prices drop, investors tend to believe output will fall, which sometimes leads to steep stock price declines.

That's why I decided to highlight three of my favorite opportunities in the REIT space of companies with stellar balance sheets, excellent operations, and clear strategic benefits that clearly show the benefits of owning American real estate.

See, the goal isn't just to present three of my favorite REITs, but also to highlight the benefits that some REITs present and their attractive valuations.

I am also using the opportunity to discuss three super important developments in the real estate world.

So, even if you don't care about my picks, I am sure you'll get some usable data from this article.

Now, let's get to the picks!

VICI Properties (VICI) - When Income Meets Resilience In A Very Tough Market

Las Vegas is in trouble. At least for now.

The city is dealing with the steepest decline in visitor numbers since the Great Financial Crisis, as the chart below shows quite well.

According to the latest numbers from the Las Vegas Convention and Visitors Authority, June saw 11.3% fewer visitors, 10.7% lower convention attendance, a 6.5 points drop in hotel occupancy rates despite fewer available rooms (i.e., the closure of the Mirage and Tropicana), and a decline of nearly 14.0% in revenue per available room.

Also, looking at the charts in the overview below, it's fair to say that things have gone south more quickly.

On top of that, its real estate market is being pressured. Below is a screenshot from Zillow I posted on X (formerly known as Twitter) the other day. It shows homes available for sale in Summerlin, which is one of the fancier places in the greater Las Vegas area. I went with the caption "It looks like the entire city of Summerlin, Nevada, is for sale."

I'm not bringing this up to trash Las Vegas, but because I believe this city has become the epicenter of America's problems.

  • The housing market has reached a tipping point, as red-hot demand since the pandemic is fading, and sellers are finding fewer buyers. In Las Vegas, it's fair to assume the impact is bigger, as the market saw a large influx of buyers after 2021, fueled by issues in Southern California and tax advantages. In general, many people wanted a piece of the LV market.
  • General consumer sentiment is down. As Las Vegas Strip resorts have gone from offering cheap drinks, rooms, and food to attract gamblers to focusing on margins on most major products, affordability is down. People simply don't want to pay $20 for a cocktail on the strip or $25 for fish and chips.
  • The ongoing trade war and stricter visa regulations may have kept some visitors from traveling to the U.S., although the data on this is thin.

The interesting thing is that despite these headwinds, the resilience of high-quality REITs is undeniable.

VICI Properties is a Las Vegas-focused REIT, as it generates roughly half of its rents in Sin City, where it owns assets like MGM Grand, Caesars Palace, Mandalay Bay, Park MGM, The Venetian, and many others.

As we can see below, the overwhelming majority of its rent comes from Caesars Entertainment (CZR), MGM Resorts (MGM), and The Venetian, which is managed by Apollo Global Management (APO), a major private lender that has found value in America's biggest gambling and entertainment market.

While Las Vegas may have its issues, the risks for VICI remain low, as its mega tenants won't close resorts just to adjust to a temporary decline in demand.

Caesars and MGM, for example, pay a combined rent bill of more than $2.2 billion every single year. They rely on their resorts. It's not like a small donut shop that can move to a different part of town if it suddenly wants to.

Moreover, VICI has average lease terms of roughly 40 years, 100% occupancy, almost every penny of rent income protected by CPI-linked boosters, and highly differentiated assets.

During its 2Q25 earnings call, VICI noted that high-end properties maintain over 90% occupancy, with operators adjusting to attract budget-conscious consumers. I expect this to continue, as the margin-focused approach simply didn't work for operators.

The company also highlighted Las Vegas's evolution toward experiential offerings like day clubs, elevated dining, sports, and innovations targeting millennials and Gen Z, who now dominate visitor share.

All of this will take time, but Las Vegas will be fine. What matters is that while these adjustments may hurt operators, they won't hurt VICI Properties.

Moreover, VICI has a balance sheet with a 5.1x leverage ratio, close to the lower bound of its 5.0x-5.5x target range, supported by $2.9 billion in liquidity and a favorable outlook, as the company raised its per-share 2025 AFFO (adjusted funds from operations) target range to $2.35-$2.37 (from $2.33 to $2.36). This shows the benefits of owning high-quality real estate even in a tough market.

It has an investment-grade credit rating of BBB-.

Currently yielding 5.4%, the company has a five-year CAGR of 7.8%, protected by a 73% payout ratio.

Going forward, analysts see 3-4% annual per-share AFFO growth. When adding its dividend and the fact that VICI trades at 14.0x AFFO, roughly 1.7 points below its long-term average, I see a path to 13-14% annual returns if REITs start to rebound.

Without valuation tailwinds, it could still return nearly 10%.

Equity LifeStyle Properties (ELS) - Housing Has Issues, This REIT Does Not

Housing has major issues in the U.S. It's not just Summerlin in Nevada.

The aforementioned mix of high home prices, elevated rates, and poor consumer sentiment has made buying a house a far-fetched dream for many.

As we can see below, housing affordability is even worse than during the onset of the Great Financial Crisis. This has pushed the median age of a first-time homebuyer to nearly 39 years. Two decades ago, half of all buyers had closed their first deal before turning 32.

Despite supply shortages, homebuilder sentiment has hit a three-year low, as macroeconomic developments have become a huge burden. Needless to say, the longer the new housing supply remains subdued, the harder it becomes to improve affordability.

This is great for Equity LifeStyle Properties, a REIT that has been very high on my watchlist for years, as I believe the downtrend in REITs makes ELS one of the best long-term investments an investor can make.

I consider ELS a superior housing REIT, as it owns high-quality manufactured housing ("MH") communities, most of which are age-restricted. It also owns RV communities and marinas, all of which enjoy favorable supply and secular tailwinds. Its total asset base covers 455 properties and roughly 173,000 sites.

Even better, the constrained supply of new MH sites supports the value of ELS's communities, especially in high-demand markets like Florida, California, and Arizona, where the company has seen consistent home sales and high occupancy rates of more than 94%.

The overwhelming majority of its "tenants" own MH houses, which means they just pay monthly fees and take care of their own assets. This is favorable for ELS, as it lowers tenant turnover and makes maintenance much easier.

It also helps that the average monthly costs of an MH asset are 66% lower than those of a single-family home, mainly due to lower housing expenses. While an MH community isn't a suitable option for every potential SFH buyer, it is a market that has seen significant improvements in quality and notable cost advantages.

This also explains why the company has never had a negative year of NOI growth and favorable inflation protection that has allowed it to generate above-inflation NOI in every macro environment since at least 2001.

Thanks to efficient management, the company has grown its per-share AFFO by 8% since 2006. During this period, it has boosted its dividend by 20% per year. The cherry on top is that the average REIT grew its per-share CAGR of just 3.8% during this period.

Currently yielding 3.5%, the REIT has a five-year dividend CAGR of 8.9% and a 79% payout ratio. Looking ahead, analysts expect AFFO to accelerate to 6% per share growth in 2027, implying a 10% annual return without valuation benefits.

Although the REIT has underperformed its peers since the pandemic, I expect a bottom in the relative performance and long-term alpha in the years ahead.

The next REIT is a bit more cyclical, yet no less impressive.

Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR) - The Industrial Real Estate Comeback

Currently, Rexford is my only REIT holding, a stock I bought a lot more of during the April sell-off, when I was able to add at less than $32 per share.

As I have written in prior articles, REXR stands out to me due to its "mission-critical" footprint in Southern California, where it owns 100% of its 422 industrial properties, giving it a market share of roughly 3% and a lot of room for off-market deals and value-adding projects due to the market's elevated average building age.

Although some people like to avoid SoCal for political reasons, I decided to mainly focus on its benefits, which include one of the best supply situations in all of North America, as SoCal is wedged between the ocean and mountains.

It is also home to America's biggest consumer base, its largest industrial market, and the San Pedro Port Complex, which handles close to a third of all U.S. port volumes, most of which remain in SocCal.

Rexford caters to companies focused on keeping SoCal's economy running, including warehouses, trade companies, general e-commerce, aerospace, manufacturing, defense, healthcare, and more.

Unfortunately, its stock price has been in a steep downtrend since

This downtrend was fueled by rising rates, new supply, and general economic worries. Even worse, this year's trade worries caused investors to price in tremendous weakness in SoCal's economy due to dependence on trade.

While I didn't enjoy these headwinds, I used them to boost my position.

Now, it seems investors are being rewarded.

While market rents across the company's portfolio declined by 12.8% year-over-year in the second quarter, the market is beginning to find a bottom thanks to easing supply headwinds. Currently, leasing activity accounts for 80% of the company's vacant space, up from 60% in 2024.

Cushman & Wakefield agrees, as their outlook sees a bottom in pricing and vacancy rates:

Vacancy increases will be modest, peaking in the high-7% range by 2026 before improving in 2027. Slower speculative construction will help limit vacancy growth, with 2025 deliveries down 46% from 2024 and falling further in 2026.

Rent growth is expected to decelerate, dipping below 2% by late 2025. As market conditions improve and supply and demand rebalance in late 2026, rent growth is projected to rebound toward the historical 3–4% range. - Cushman & Wakefield

And even before an upswing, Rexford remains in a good spot, as embedded growth remains strong at $195 million in incremental cash NOI, which is the equivalent to 28% growth, with $105 million coming from contractual rent steps, $70 million from ongoing repositioning and redevelopment projects, and $20 million from a 3% cash mark-to-market.

And, just like the other two REITs in this article, the balance sheet is strong, with $1.8 billion in liquidity, including $560 million in cash, and net debt to EBITDA at just 4.0x. It has a BBB+ credit rating, one step below the A range.

It currently yields 4.4%. This dividend has a five-year CAGR of 16.2%. Although I expect dividend growth to remain in the mid-single-digit range.

The company, which has an 81% payout ratio, is expected to grow its per-share AFFO by 3-5% per year in 2025-2027. I expect substantially higher growth if we see higher inflation (mark-to-market benefits) and broader economic growth (real estate demand).

Applying a 25x multiple, in line with peer multiples like Prologis (PLD), I see a fair stock price of at least $55-$60, roughly 40-50% above the current price.

Given the bottoming process in industrial real estate and REITs, in general, I will remain a buyer of REXR shares on weakness.

Takeaway

While the headlines scream about affordability crises and poor sentiment, I see a tremendous opportunity in what has become my favorite place to buy safe income for the next decade (and beyond).

To me, the data is clear.

Foreign investors are rushing back into U.S. real estate for a reason, and REITs are historically cheap, trading at their biggest discount to the market since the Financial Crisis.

I think it's fair to say this isn't just a statistical anomaly but a great setup for a period of potential outperformance.

The picks in this article each have a different facet of American real estate resilience, including irreplaceable assets, secular housing demand, and a mission-critical industrial footprint.

The goal isn't to time the bottom but to recognize that when high-quality assets with stellar balance sheets are on sale, the long-term odds are in our favor.

Risks To My Thesis

While all REITs mentioned in this article are "safe," the biggest risks are a steep recession for Rexford, as its occupancy rate risks are much higher than those of ELS and VICI.

In general, the biggest risk is prolonged underperformance if longer-term government bond rates do not come down.
